Flashcard 2.1 (original mcq)
Front: MCQ2. Comparing to the mature breast milk, colostrum has
A- Low specific gravity.
B- Equal amount of humoral immunity.
C- Low fat and carbohydrate.
D- Low protein.
E- Equal amount of minerals.
Back: C- Low fat and carbohydrate.
Flashcard 2.2
Front: How does colostrum compare to mature breast milk regarding specific gravity
and protein?
Back: Explanation : Colostrum has: 1- higher specific gravity, 2- more protein
Flashcard 2.3
Front: How does colostrum compare to mature breast milk regarding humoral
immunity?
Back: Explanation : Colostrum has: 3- more humeral immunity
Flashcard 2.4
Front: How does colostrum compare to mature breast milk regarding fat and
carbohydrate content?
Back: Explanation : Colostrum has: 4- less fat and carbohydrate than mature breast
milk
Flashcard 56.1 (original mcq)
Front: MCQ56. How long the exclusive breastfeeding is recommended
A at least up to one month
B.at least up to 3 months
C.at least up to 6 months
D.at least up to 12 months
E.at least up to 24 months
Back: C.at least up to 6 months
Flashcard 56.2
Front: For how long is exclusive breastfeeding recommended, and what is
recommended afterwards?
Back: Explanation : Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 4-6
months of life to ensure optimal growth, development, and health of the infant. After

6 months, it is recommended to continue breastfeeding along with appropriate
complementary foods
Flashcard 96.1 (original mcq)
Front: MCQ96) Human breast milk :
A. Contain less carbohydrate than cow's milk
B. Contain more iron than cow's milk
C. Supply necessary amount of vitamin K
D. HIV virus is transmitted by breast feeding
E. Supply all the necessary nutrients including fluoride and vitamin
Back: D. HIV virus is transmitted by breast feeding
Flashcard 96.2
Front: Compare Human milk vs Cow milk regarding: Carbohydrate, Protein (level
and type), Fat (amount and type), Calcium, Phosphorus, Iron.
Back: Explanation : Human milk: More carbohydrate, Less protein (mainly whey), Fat
almost equal (more unsaturated), Less calcium, phosphorus, iron. Cow milk: Less
carbohydrate, Higher protein (mainly casein), Fat almost equal (more saturated),
More calcium, phosphorus, iron.
Flashcard 143.1 (original mcq)
Front: MCQ143) 9 months old infant presents poor feeding , lethargy on
examination there is thin skin, thin hair , edema of abdomen and leg . The most likely
diagnosis is
A. Marasmus
B. Kwashiorkor
C. Sever Dehydration
D. Sepsis
Back: Answer = B
Explanation : Kwashiorkor is a form of severe malnutrition characterized by protein
deficiency. Key features include edema (swelling), an enlarged abdomen, thin and
brittle hair, and changes in skin pigmentation. The presence of edema distinguishes
it from marasmus, which typically presents with severe wasting without edema.
(Comparison table provided in image)
• Kwashiorkor: Develops in children whose diets are deficient of protein.
Occurs between 6 months and 3 years. Subcutaneous fat is preserved.
Oedema is present. Enlarged fatty liver. Ribs are not very prominent.

Lethargic. Muscle wasting mild or absent. Hypopigmented hair. Dermatosis.
Moon face. Poor appetite. Needs adequate amounts of proteins.
• Marasmus: Due to deficiency of proteins and calories. Common in infants
under 1 year. Subcutaneous fat is not preserved. Oedema is absent. No fatty
liver. Ribs become very prominent. Alert and irritable. Severe muscle
wasting. Normal hair. Xerotic, wrinkled, loose skin. Old man like face.
Voracious feeder. Needs adequate amount of protein, fats and
carbohydrates.
Flashcard 219.1 (original mcq)
Front: MCQ219) Newborn male baby present with poor feeding he is on bottle
feeding and mother state that she gave water and Cola , What the most likely
complication baby Develop
A- Hyperkalemia
B- Hypernatremia
C- Hyponatremia
D- Hypoglycemia
E- Hypercalcemia
Back: Answer = C
Explanation : Newborn baby should be on exclusive breast feeding at least for 4-6
month Their kidneys are about half the size of an adult's. So, they can't hold much
water to begin with, and it takes just a few ounces to cause problems. On top of
that, their kidneys aren't developed enough, yet, to properly filter water. So, any
water that enters their body ends up in the circulatory system, where it dilutes their
blood, and increases their water content by 7 to 8%. So this lead to water
intoxication and hyponatremia
Flashcard 1 (MCQ 242)
• Front: 242) Absolute contraindication to breast feeding is
• Options:
A- Hepatitis C
B- Alcohol
C- Chronic pulmonary TB
D- Herpes simplex infection of the breast
E- Maternal mastitis
• Back: Answer = D (Referencing explanation on next page for full list)

• Explanation: (From page 128) Absolute and relative contraindications for
breastfeeding:
1- Maternal HIV infection.
2- Tuberculosis..(until complete 2wks maternal therapy)
3- Varicella zoster
4- Herpes simplex lesion of the breast
5- CMV infection
6- Chemotherapy and radiotherapy
7- Baby galactosemia
Flashcard 2 (MCQ 244)
• Front: 244) Most common presentation of Wilson disease in children under 8
years old
• Options:
A- Ataxia
B- Arthritis
C- Seizure
D- Hemolysis
E- Liver dysfunction
• Back: Answer = E
• Explanation: Liver disease is the most common disease manifestation in
children and can precede neurologic symptoms by as long as 10 years
Flashcard 3 (MCQ 331)
• Front: 331) Which of the following is not a risk factor for the development of
asthma at school age?
• Options:
A- Allergic rhinitis
B- Breast-fed as infant.
C- Food allergies
D- Obesity
E- Parental history of asthma
• Back: Answer = B
• Explanation: Breastfeeding is generally considered protective against
various respiratory infections and may have some protective effects against

early childhood wheezing. It is not typically associated with an increased risk
of developing asthma at school age. Asthma risk was shown to be lower in
children breastfed for more than 16 weeks compared to those not breastfed.
The other factors listed - allergic rhinitis, food allergies, obesity, and parental
history of asthma are well-established risk factors for the development of
asthma during childhood.
Breastfeeding have protective effects against :
1- Diarrhea, celiac disease, Crohn disease
2- Otitis media
3- Urinary tract infection
4- Necrotizing enterocolitis , Septicemia
5- Infant botulism
6- Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
7- Childhood cancer , Lymphoma , Leukemia
8- Allergy , asthma
9- Hospitalizations and Infant mortality
Flashcard 4 (MCQ 344)
• Front: 344) Which of the following statements concerning measles and
vitamin A is false
• Options:
A- There is not a confirmed relationship of vitamin A to the prognosis of
measles
B- Measles causes vitamin A deficiency
C- Treatment with vitamin A reduces measles severity for children in
developing countries.
D- Vitamin A supplementation should be given to all children with measles
E- None of the above.
• Back: Answer = A
• Explanation: There is a well-established relationship between vitamin A
status and the prognosis of measles. Measles can lead to a depletion of
vitamin A stores in the body. Numerous studies have shown that vitamin A
supplementation can reduce the risk of complications, particularly in
children living in developing countries where vitamin A deficiency is more
prevalent the WHO and the CDC recommend that vitamin A be administered
to all children with acute measles, even in countries where measles is not

usually severe.
Vitamin A therapy is indicated for all patients with measles. Vitamin A should
be administered once daily for 2 days at doses of
1- 200,000 IU for children 12 mo of age or older
2- 100,000 IU for infants 6 mo through 11 mo of age
3- 50,000 IU for infants younger than 6 mo of age.
In children with signs and symptoms of vitamin A deficiency, a 3rd age-
appropriate dose is recommended 2-4 wk after the 2nd dose
Flashcard 5 (MCQ 347)
• Front: 347) All of the following statements regarding iron deficiency in
children are true Except
• Options:
A- Because absorption of dietary iron is assumed to be about 10%, a diet
containing 80-100 mg of iron daily is necessary for optimal nutrition.
B- Intense exercise conditioning may result in iron depletion in adolescent
girls
C- Iron deficiency may have effects on neurologic and intellectual function.
D- The level of serum ferritin provides a relatively accurate estimate of body
iron stores in the absence of inflammatory disease
E- The red cell distribution width (RDW) is elevated in iron deficiency but not
in thalassemia trait
• Back: Answer = A
• Explanation: The recommended daily dietary iron intake for children is much
lower. For example, the recommended daily intake for toddlers aged 1-3
years is about 7 mg, and for children aged 4-8 years, it is about 10 mg.
Adolescents have higher requirements, but still far less than 80-100 mg per
day. The assumption of 10% absorption is generally correct, but the
necessary dietary intake is much lower than stated.
Flashcard 1 (Q371)
Question: Vitamin C deficiency is associated with all of the following except
A- The early symptoms include low-grade fever, irritability
B- Bone pain result in pseudo paralysis
C- Patient may present with scurvy syndrome
D- in older children and adolescents there is swollen joints, purpura, ecchymosis

E- Daily vitamin C supplement 30 mg orally or parenterally may insure more rapid
cure
Answer: E
Explanation: The early manifestations of vitamin C deficiency are irritability, loss of
appetite, low-grade fever, musculoskeletal pain, and tenderness in the legs. These
signs and symptoms are followed by leg swelling—most marked at the knees and
the ankles—and pseudoparalysis. Vitamin C supplements of 100-200 mg/day orally
or parenterally ensure rapid and complete cure. The clinical improvement is seen
within 1 week in most cases, but the treatment should be continued for up to 3 mo
for complete recovery. (A 30mg dose is insufficient for treatment).
Flashcard 2 (Q376)
Question: You are counseling a new mother who is thinking that her breast milk is
not suitable for her child. Which of the following is NOT true regarding breast milk?
A- Carbohydrate content is lactose
B- Fat content is cholesterol, triglycerides, essential free fatty acids (up to 50%
energy from fat)
C- Gives energy 67 kcal/100 ml.
D- Iron content is of higher bioavailability and it meets iron requirements for first 12
months
E- Protein content is mainly whey (80%) which is more easily digested than casein
Answer: D
Explanation: Breast milk indeed has low iron content, and while it is highly
bioavailable, it may not meet the iron requirements for the first 6 months of life,
particularly as the infant grows and their iron needs increase. Therefore, it is
recommended to introduce iron-rich complementary foods around 6 months of age
and consider iron supplementation by age of 4-6 months.
Flashcard 3 (Q387)
Question: Vitamin B12 deficiency is most likely to occur in a child with
A- Resection of the jejunum
B- Resection of the ileum
C- Resection of the colon

D- A colostomy
E- A gastrojejunostomy
Answer: B
Explanation: Causes of vitamin B12 Deficiency:
1- inadequate dietary intake occurs primarily in persons consuming strict vegetarian
or vegan diets
2- Breastfeeding infants of B12-deficient mothers are also at risk for significant
deficiency.
3- Malabsorption of B12 occurs in celiac disease, ileal resections, Crohn disease,
Helicobacter pylori infection, and
4- autoimmune atrophic gastritis (pernicious anemia). Use of metformin, proton
pump inhibitors, and histamine (H2) receptor antagonists may increase the risk of
deficiency.
5- Hereditary intrinsic factor deficiency and Imerslund-Gräsbeck disease.
(The ileum is the primary site of Vitamin B12 absorption).
Flashcard 4 (Q390)
Question: Which vitamin deficiency will lead to Beriberi in child
A. Vitamin B1
B- Vitamin B2
C- vitamin B5
D- Vitamin B6
E- Vitamin B12
Answer: A
Explanation: Thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency can develop within 2-3 mo of a
deficient intake. Early symptoms of thiamine deficiency are nonspecific, such as
fatigue, apathy, irritability, depression, drowsiness, poor mental concentration,
anorexia, nausea, and abdominal discomfort. As the condition progresses, more-
specific manifestations of beriberi develop, such as peripheral neuritis, heart
failure, and psychologic disturbances.
Flashcard 5 (Q401)

Question: A 5 year old boy is brought to his pediatrician by his parent with
compliant of three times wrist fracture without history of trauma, weaknes and
abnormal gait. On physical examination found to have bowed legs, stunted growth
and bone tenderness of lower limbs. Blood test was significant for low calcium, low
phosphorus, high alkaline phosphatase, normal 25 hydroxyvitamin D and low 1,25-
dihydroxyvitamin D3. The most important treatment include one of the following
A. Parathyroid Hormone
B. Phosphate supplementation
C. Ergocalciferol (25-hydroxycholecalciferol]
D. Calcitriol(1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol]
E. Calcuim supplementation
Answer: D
Explanation: A case of vitamin D-dependent rickets type 1A, an autosomal
recessive disorder, have mutations in the gene encoding renal 1α-hydroxylase,
preventing conversion of 25-D into 1,25-D. These patients normally present during
the 1st 2 yr of life and can have any of the classic features of rickets including
symptomatic hypocalcemia. They have normal levels of 25-D but low levels of 1,25-
D. Occasionally, 1,25-D levels are at the lower limit of normal. Vitamin D-dependent
rickets type 1A responds to long-term treatment with 1,25-D (calcitriol). Initial doses
are 0.25-2 µ g/day , and lower doses are used once the rickets has healed.
Flashcard 6 (Q402 & Q420 - Table)
Question: A 2-year-old boy presents to the pediatrician for a well-child visit...
notable for bowed legs [Photo provided]. Laboratory values: Serum Ca++: 9.1
mg/dL, Phosphorus: 1.1 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase: 462 U/L, Parathyroid
hormone: 75 pg/mL (N = 10-55), 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D was high with normal 25
hydroxyvitamin D. What is the most likely diagnosis
A. Pseudohypoparathyroidism
B. Renal osteodystrophy
C. Vitamin D deficiency rickets
D. Vitamin D resistant rickets
E. Type I vitamin D dependent rickets
Answer: D
Explanation: Based on the laboratory findings and clinical presentation, the most
likely diagnosis is Vitamin D resistant rickets. This condition is characterized by:

• Normal serum calcium levels
• Low serum phosphorus levels
• Elevated alkaline phosphatase
• Elevated parathyroid hormone
• Elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels with normal 25-hydroxyvitamin D
levels.
(The table on page 209 confirms these findings for Vitamin D resistant rickets
type 2 and X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets). Vitamin D-Resistant Rickets
(specifically Type II) involves a defect in the vitamin D receptor's ability to
respond to calcitriol.
Flashcard 7 (Q433)
Question: A 1-week-old term female infant suddenly develops melena. The mother
is healthy, and the pregnancy was normal, Delivery was at home, but it was
uncomplicated The infant is breastfeeding Hemoglobin is 6.7 gdl. (67gL), white
blood cell count is 13,000'cu/mm (13 x 109L), and platelets are 599 x 103/eu mm
(599 x 109/L). the MOST likely cause of the bleeding is:
A. factor V deficiency hemophilia
B. factor X deficiency hemophilia
C. necrotizing enterocolitis
D. rectal fissure
E. vitamin K deficiency
Answer: E
Explanation: Classic vitamin K deficiency can present with melena, large
cephalohematomas, intracranial hemorrhage, and bleeding from the umbilical
stump, injection sites, and after circumcision. Bleeding occurs most commonly
from the second to seventh days of life in healthy, breastfed, and often term infants
who have not received prophylactic vitamin K. It occurs rarely in formula-fed infants
because of the supplemental vitamin K in those products. Early vitamin K deficiency
(within the first 24 hours of life) is linked to maternal use of medications (most often
anticonvulsants). Late vitamin K deficiency (2 to 8 weeks of age) is linked to a
compromised supply (diarrhea, cystic fibrosis, hepatitis, celiac disease). Home
birth increases risk if prophylactic Vitamin K1 (phytonadione) injection is not
administered.

Flashcard 8 (Q436)
Question: A 1-month-old girl hospitalized for acute gastroenteritis caused by
rotavirus. Her diarrhea had decreased in the hospital while taking oral rehydration
solution, but when her mother resumed her usual cow milk formula, the girl began
to have an increased number of very watery stools. She appears well hydrated, and
findings on her abdominal examination are normal, the MOST appropriate approach
to managing this infant's diarrhea is to
A. change to a lactose-free formula for the next few days.
B. dilute the cow milk formula with oral rehydration solution for the next few days
C. gives her only oral rehydration solution until the diarrhea resolves
D. readmits her to the hospital for administration of intravenous fluids
E. repeats her stool studies to confirm the diagnosis of rotavirus infection
Answer: A
Explanation: Acute gastroenteritis that is severe enough to cause intestinal injury
(often due to rotavirus infection) is a common cause of transient lactose intolerance
in young children. The symptoms are often not clinically relevant. Treatment
recommending the temporary elimination of lactose from the diet.
Flashcard 9 (Q458 - Related to Q402 Table)
Question: A 3-year-old toddler is seen for bowlegs. His blood work reveals: Serum
calcium-8.5 mg/dl, Phosphate 3 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase-850 U/dL. All of the
following statements are true regarding the etiology of the toddler's diagnosis
except:
a. Anticonvulsant therapy is a recognized cause
b. Cystic fibrosis is a recognized cause
c. inadequate exposure to the sun may cause this condition
d. This condition is rare in low-birth-weight infants and in adolescents.
e. Chronic renal failure is a recognized cause
Answer: D
Explanation: The toddler's clinical presentation and blood work suggest rickets
(low/normal Ca, low Phos, high ALP). Rickets can result from several underlying
conditions, including nutritional deficiencies, malabsorption (CF), lack of sun
exposure, certain medications (anticonvulsants), and renal disease. Rickets can be

seen in low-birth-weight infants, and Higher incidence of postnatal rickets is seen in
infants with fetal growth restriction. It can also occur during adolescence, especially
during growth spurts when nutritional needs are not met adequately. Therefore, the
statement that it's rare in these groups is incorrect.
Flashcard 10 (Q504)
Question: Regarding the daily nutritional requirements of 10 months old infant of 10
kg body weight all the following are true EXCEPT:
a. 1200ml of water.
b. 4000 IU of vitamin D.
c. 10 mg iron.
d. 1200 Kcal.
e. 1000 IU of vitamin A.
Answer: B
Explanation: The recommendation for vitamin D intake in infants (from birth to 12
months) is 400 IU per day. This amount is sufficient to ensure proper bone health
and to prevent conditions like rickets. Vitamin D helps in the absorption of calcium
and phosphate, which are essential for healthy bone development. Doses as high as
4000 IU/day significantly exceed the safe upper limit for infants. Excessive vitamin D
can lead to hypercalcemia.
Flashcard 11 (Q510)
Question: A child develop oxaluria The most likely diagnosis is
a Vitamin A excess
b Vitamin C excess
c Vitamin D excess
d. Vitamin K excess
e Vitamin E excess
Answer: B
Explanation: Oxaluria is the presence of oxalate in the urine and can be associated
with several conditions. Secondary hyperoxaluria is more common and can occur in
patients with increased intake of oxalate and oxalate precursors such as vitamin C,
in those with pyridoxine deficiency, and in children with intestinal malabsorption.

Flashcard 12 (Q513)
Question: A severely malnourished child develops seizure activities during recovery
from diarrhea. seizure may stop with the following therapy except
a. Calcium
b. Magnesium
c. Glucose
d. Sodium
e. Protein
Answer: E
Explanation: Although malnutrition can cause hypoproteinemia and edema,
Protein is not directly related to the pathophysiology of seizure. The other electrolyte
disturbances (hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia) can
cause seizure and are reported as complication of malnutrition.
Flashcard 1 (Q546):
• Question: The followings are possible clinical signs of malnutrition. Except:
o a. Bradycardia
o b. Normal development.
o c. Loss of knee and ankle reflexes
o d. Chvostek or Trousseau sign
o e. Hepatomegaly with fatty liver.
• Answer: B
• Explanation: In cases of malnutrition, especially prolonged malnutrition, we
typically see impaired growth and developmental delays. Therefore, "normal
development" would not be consistent with malnutrition. (See also clinical
signs table provided in OCR page 283).
Flashcard 2 (Q550):
• Question: A mother wishes to breast-feed her newborn infant, but is worried
about medical conditions that would prohibit her from doing so. You counsel
her that of her listed conditions, which of the following is a contraindication
to breast-feeding?

o a. Upper respiratory tract infection
o b. Cracked and bleeding nipples
o c. Mastitis
o d. Inverted nipples
o e. HIV infection
• Answer: E
• Explanation: HIV infection is a contraindication to breastfeeding in
developed countries. The risk of transmitting HIV through breast milk poses a
significant concern, and alternative feeding methods are recommended.
Flashcard 3 (Q568):
• Question: Which of the following is most common cause of Uncojugated
non hemolytic neonatal Jaundice
o A. Hypothyroidism
o B. Pyloric stenosis
o C. Breast milk Jaundice
o D. Gilbert syndrome
• Answer: C
• Explanation: Breast milk jaundice typically appears in the second week of
life and can persist for several weeks. It is caused by substances in breast
milk that inhibit the conjugation of bilirubin or increase enterohepatic
circulation of bilirubin. Gilbert syndrome is another cause of Uncojugated
non hemolytic neonatal Jaundice but it mostly presents in the second
decade of life with only few patients present with neonatal periods.
Flashcard 4 (Q583):
• Question: Characteristic features of kwashiorkor include all the following
except
o A-Moon face
o B-Dermatitis
o C-Sparse hair

o D-Voracious appetite
• Answer: D
• Explanation: Kwashiorkor is a form of severe protein-energy malnutrition. It
is characterized by several clinical features, but a voracious appetite is not
one of them. In fact, children with kwashiorkor often exhibit anorexia (lack of
appetite) rather than a voracious appetite. (See also clinical signs table
provided in OCR page 283).
Flashcard 5 (Q589):
• Question: All the following are causes of Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
Except
o A. Physiological Jaundice
o B. Breastfeeding jaundice
o C. ABO incompatibility
o D. Biliary atresia
• Answer: D
• Explanation: Biliary atresia is a condition that causes conjugated
hyperbilirubinemia. In biliary atresia, the bile ducts are malformed or absent,
which leads to a blockage in bile flow. This results in the accumulation of
conjugated bilirubin in the blood, causing direct (conjugated)
hyperbilirubinemia. Breastfeeding jaundice causes unconjugated
hyperbilirubinemia.
Flashcard 6 (Q618):
• Question: Regarding nutritional rickets all the following are true Except :
o A. Low s.calcium, high s. Phosphate
o B. There is Thickening of wrist
o C. Bow leg is recognized feature
o D. Softening of the ribs may predisposes patients to atelectasis and
pneumonia
• Answer: A

• Explanation: In nutritional rickets, there is typically low serum calcium and
low or normal serum phosphate due to impaired vitamin D metabolism,
which affects calcium and phosphate absorption. Therefore, high serum
phosphate is not characteristic. Thickening of the wrists and bow legs are
classic physical findings in rickets. Softening of the ribs (rachitic rosary) can
predispose children to atelectasis and pneumonia due to the structural
weakness in the chest wall.

early childhood wheezing. It is not typically associated with an increased risk
of developing asthma at school age. Asthma risk was shown to be lower in
children breastfed for more than 16 weeks compared to those not breastfed.
The other factors listed - allergic rhinitis, food allergies, obesity, and parental
history of asthma are well-established risk factors for the development of
asthma during childhood.
Breastfeeding have protective effects against :
1- Diarrhea, celiac disease, Crohn disease
2- Otitis media
3- Urinary tract infection
4- Necrotizing enterocolitis , Septicemia
5- Infant botulism
6- Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
7- Childhood cancer , Lymphoma , Leukemia
8- Allergy , asthma
9- Hospitalizations and Infant mortality
Flashcard 4 (MCQ 344)
• Front: 344) Which of the following statements concerning measles and
vitamin A is false
• Options:
A- There is not a confirmed relationship of vitamin A to the prognosis of
measles
B- Measles causes vitamin A deficiency
C- Treatment with vitamin A reduces measles severity for children in
developing countries.
D- Vitamin A supplementation should be given to all children with measles
E- None of the above.
• Back: Answer = A
• Explanation: There is a well-established relationship between vitamin A
status and the prognosis of measles. Measles can lead to a depletion of
vitamin A stores in the body. Numerous studies have shown that vitamin A
supplementation can reduce the risk of complications, particularly in
children living in developing countries where vitamin A deficiency is more
prevalent the WHO and the CDC recommend that vitamin A be administered
to all children with acute measles, even in countries where measles is not
--- Page 6 ---
usually severe.
Vitamin A therapy is indicated for all patients with measles. Vitamin A should
be administered once daily for 2 days at doses of
1- 200,000 IU for children 12 mo of age or older
2- 100,000 IU for infants 6 mo through 11 mo of age
3- 50,000 IU for infants younger than 6 mo of age.
In children with signs and symptoms of vitamin A deficiency, a 3rd age-
appropriate dose is recommended 2-4 wk after the 2nd dose
Flashcard 5 (MCQ 347)
• Front: 347) All of the following statements regarding iron deficiency in
children are true Except
• Options:
A- Because absorption of dietary iron is assumed to be about 10%, a diet
containing 80-100 mg of iron daily is necessary for optimal nutrition.
B- Intense exercise conditioning may result in iron depletion in adolescent
girls
C- Iron deficiency may have effects on neurologic and intellectual function.
D- The level of serum ferritin provides a relatively accurate estimate of body
iron stores in the absence of inflammatory disease
E- The red cell distribution width (RDW) is elevated in iron deficiency but not
in thalassemia trait
• Back: Answer = A
• Explanation: The recommended daily dietary iron intake for children is much
lower. For example, the recommended daily intake for toddlers aged 1-3
years is about 7 mg, and for children aged 4-8 years, it is about 10 mg.
Adolescents have higher requirements, but still far less than 80-100 mg per
day. The assumption of 10% absorption is generally correct, but the
necessary dietary intake is much lower than stated.
Flashcard 1 (Q371)
Question: Vitamin C deficiency is associated with all of the following except
A- The early symptoms include low-grade fever, irritability
B- Bone pain result in pseudo paralysis
C- Patient may present with scurvy syndrome
D- in older children and adolescents there is swollen joints, purpura, ecchymosis
--- Page 7 ---
E- Daily vitamin C supplement 30 mg orally or parenterally may insure more rapid
cure
Answer: E
Explanation: The early manifestations of vitamin C deficiency are irritability, loss of
appetite, low-grade fever, musculoskeletal pain, and tenderness in the legs. These
signs and symptoms are followed by leg swelling—most marked at the knees and
the ankles—and pseudoparalysis. Vitamin C supplements of 100-200 mg/day orally
or parenterally ensure rapid and complete cure. The clinical improvement is seen
within 1 week in most cases, but the treatment should be continued for up to 3 mo
for complete recovery. (A 30mg dose is insufficient for treatment).
Flashcard 2 (Q376)
Question: You are counseling a new mother who is thinking that her breast milk is
not suitable for her child. Which of the following is NOT true regarding breast milk?
A- Carbohydrate content is lactose
B- Fat content is cholesterol, triglycerides, essential free fatty acids (up to 50%
energy from fat)
C- Gives energy 67 kcal/100 ml.
D- Iron content is of higher bioavailability and it meets iron requirements for first 12
months
E- Protein content is mainly whey (80%) which is more easily digested than casein
Answer: D
Explanation: Breast milk indeed has low iron content, and while it is highly
bioavailable, it may not meet the iron requirements for the first 6 months of life,
particularly as the infant grows and their iron needs increase. Therefore, it is
recommended to introduce iron-rich complementary foods around 6 months of age
and consider iron supplementation by age of 4-6 months.
Flashcard 3 (Q387)
Question: Vitamin B12 deficiency is most likely to occur in a child with
A- Resection of the jejunum
B- Resection of the ileum
C- Resection of the colon
--- Page 8 ---
D- A colostomy
E- A gastrojejunostomy
Answer: B
Explanation: Causes of vitamin B12 Deficiency:
1- inadequate dietary intake occurs primarily in persons consuming strict vegetarian
or vegan diets
2- Breastfeeding infants of B12-deficient mothers are also at risk for significant
deficiency.
3- Malabsorption of B12 occurs in celiac disease, ileal resections, Crohn disease,
Helicobacter pylori infection, and
4- autoimmune atrophic gastritis (pernicious anemia). Use of metformin, proton
pump inhibitors, and histamine (H2) receptor antagonists may increase the risk of
deficiency.
5- Hereditary intrinsic factor deficiency and Imerslund-Gräsbeck disease.
(The ileum is the primary site of Vitamin B12 absorption).
Flashcard 4 (Q390)
Question: Which vitamin deficiency will lead to Beriberi in child
A. Vitamin B1
B- Vitamin B2
C- vitamin B5
D- Vitamin B6
E- Vitamin B12
Answer: A
Explanation: Thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency can develop within 2-3 mo of a
deficient intake. Early symptoms of thiamine deficiency are nonspecific, such as
fatigue, apathy, irritability, depression, drowsiness, poor mental concentration,
anorexia, nausea, and abdominal discomfort. As the condition progresses, more-
specific manifestations of beriberi develop, such as peripheral neuritis, heart
failure, and psychologic disturbances.
Flashcard 5 (Q401)
--- Page 9 ---
Question: A 5 year old boy is brought to his pediatrician by his parent with
compliant of three times wrist fracture without history of trauma, weaknes and
abnormal gait. On physical examination found to have bowed legs, stunted growth
and bone tenderness of lower limbs. Blood test was significant for low calcium, low
phosphorus, high alkaline phosphatase, normal 25 hydroxyvitamin D and low 1,25-
dihydroxyvitamin D3. The most important treatment include one of the following
A. Parathyroid Hormone
B. Phosphate supplementation
C. Ergocalciferol (25-hydroxycholecalciferol]
D. Calcitriol(1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol]
E. Calcuim supplementation
Answer: D
Explanation: A case of vitamin D-dependent rickets type 1A, an autosomal
recessive disorder, have mutations in the gene encoding renal 1α-hydroxylase,
preventing conversion of 25-D into 1,25-D. These patients normally present during
the 1st 2 yr of life and can have any of the classic features of rickets including
symptomatic hypocalcemia. They have normal levels of 25-D but low levels of 1,25-
D. Occasionally, 1,25-D levels are at the lower limit of normal. Vitamin D-dependent
rickets type 1A responds to long-term treatment with 1,25-D (calcitriol). Initial doses
are 0.25-2 µ g/day , and lower doses are used once the rickets has healed.
Flashcard 6 (Q402 & Q420 - Table)
Question: A 2-year-old boy presents to the pediatrician for a well-child visit...
notable for bowed legs [Photo provided]. Laboratory values: Serum Ca++: 9.1
mg/dL, Phosphorus: 1.1 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase: 462 U/L, Parathyroid
hormone: 75 pg/mL (N = 10-55), 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D was high with normal 25
hydroxyvitamin D. What is the most likely diagnosis
A. Pseudohypoparathyroidism
B. Renal osteodystrophy
C. Vitamin D deficiency rickets
D. Vitamin D resistant rickets
E. Type I vitamin D dependent rickets
Answer: D
Explanation: Based on the laboratory findings and clinical presentation, the most
likely diagnosis is Vitamin D resistant rickets. This condition is characterized by:
--- Page 10 ---
• Normal serum calcium levels
• Low serum phosphorus levels
• Elevated alkaline phosphatase
• Elevated parathyroid hormone
• Elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels with normal 25-hydroxyvitamin D
levels.
(The table on page 209 confirms these findings for Vitamin D resistant rickets
type 2 and X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets). Vitamin D-Resistant Rickets
(specifically Type II) involves a defect in the vitamin D receptor's ability to
respond to calcitriol.
Flashcard 7 (Q433)
Question: A 1-week-old term female infant suddenly develops melena. The mother
is healthy, and the pregnancy was normal, Delivery was at home, but it was
uncomplicated The infant is breastfeeding Hemoglobin is 6.7 gdl. (67gL), white
blood cell count is 13,000'cu/mm (13 x 109L), and platelets are 599 x 103/eu mm
(599 x 109/L). the MOST likely cause of the bleeding is:
A. factor V deficiency hemophilia
B. factor X deficiency hemophilia
C. necrotizing enterocolitis
D. rectal fissure
E. vitamin K deficiency
Answer: E
Explanation: Classic vitamin K deficiency can present with melena, large
cephalohematomas, intracranial hemorrhage, and bleeding from the umbilical
stump, injection sites, and after circumcision. Bleeding occurs most commonly
from the second to seventh days of life in healthy, breastfed, and often term infants
who have not received prophylactic vitamin K. It occurs rarely in formula-fed infants
because of the supplemental vitamin K in those products. Early vitamin K deficiency
(within the first 24 hours of life) is linked to maternal use of medications (most often
anticonvulsants). Late vitamin K deficiency (2 to 8 weeks of age) is linked to a
compromised supply (diarrhea, cystic fibrosis, hepatitis, celiac disease). Home
birth increases risk if prophylactic Vitamin K1 (phytonadione) injection is not
administered.
--- Page 11 ---
Flashcard 8 (Q436)
Question: A 1-month-old girl hospitalized for acute gastroenteritis caused by
rotavirus. Her diarrhea had decreased in the hospital while taking oral rehydration
solution, but when her mother resumed her usual cow milk formula, the girl began
to have an increased number of very watery stools. She appears well hydrated, and
findings on her abdominal examination are normal, the MOST appropriate approach
to managing this infant's diarrhea is to
A. change to a lactose-free formula for the next few days.
B. dilute the cow milk formula with oral rehydration solution for the next few days
C. gives her only oral rehydration solution until the diarrhea resolves
D. readmits her to the hospital for administration of intravenous fluids
E. repeats her stool studies to confirm the diagnosis of rotavirus infection
Answer: A
Explanation: Acute gastroenteritis that is severe enough to cause intestinal injury
(often due to rotavirus infection) is a common cause of transient lactose intolerance
in young children. The symptoms are often not clinically relevant. Treatment
recommending the temporary elimination of lactose from the diet.
Flashcard 9 (Q458 - Related to Q402 Table)
Question: A 3-year-old toddler is seen for bowlegs. His blood work reveals: Serum
calcium-8.5 mg/dl, Phosphate 3 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase-850 U/dL. All of the
following statements are true regarding the etiology of the toddler's diagnosis
except:
a. Anticonvulsant therapy is a recognized cause
b. Cystic fibrosis is a recognized cause
c. inadequate exposure to the sun may cause this condition
d. This condition is rare in low-birth-weight infants and in adolescents.
e. Chronic renal failure is a recognized cause
Answer: D
Explanation: The toddler's clinical presentation and blood work suggest rickets
(low/normal Ca, low Phos, high ALP). Rickets can result from several underlying
conditions, including nutritional deficiencies, malabsorption (CF), lack of sun
exposure, certain medications (anticonvulsants), and renal disease. Rickets can be
--- Page 12 ---
seen in low-birth-weight infants, and Higher incidence of postnatal rickets is seen in
infants with fetal growth restriction. It can also occur during adolescence, especially
during growth spurts when nutritional needs are not met adequately. Therefore, the
statement that it's rare in these groups is incorrect.
Flashcard 10 (Q504)
Question: Regarding the daily nutritional requirements of 10 months old infant of 10
kg body weight all the following are true EXCEPT:
a. 1200ml of water.
b. 4000 IU of vitamin D.
c. 10 mg iron.
d. 1200 Kcal.
e. 1000 IU of vitamin A.
Answer: B
Explanation: The recommendation for vitamin D intake in infants (from birth to 12
months) is 400 IU per day. This amount is sufficient to ensure proper bone health
and to prevent conditions like rickets. Vitamin D helps in the absorption of calcium
and phosphate, which are essential for healthy bone development. Doses as high as
4000 IU/day significantly exceed the safe upper limit for infants. Excessive vitamin D
can lead to hypercalcemia.
Flashcard 11 (Q510)
Question: A child develop oxaluria The most likely diagnosis is
a Vitamin A excess
b Vitamin C excess
c Vitamin D excess
d. Vitamin K excess
e Vitamin E excess
Answer: B
Explanation: Oxaluria is the presence of oxalate in the urine and can be associated
with several conditions. Secondary hyperoxaluria is more common and can occur in
patients with increased intake of oxalate and oxalate precursors such as vitamin C,
in those with pyridoxine deficiency, and in children with intestinal malabsorption.
--- Page 13 ---
Flashcard 12 (Q513)
Question: A severely malnourished child develops seizure activities during recovery
from diarrhea. seizure may stop with the following therapy except
a. Calcium
b. Magnesium
c. Glucose
d. Sodium
e. Protein
Answer: E
Explanation: Although malnutrition can cause hypoproteinemia and edema,
Protein is not directly related to the pathophysiology of seizure. The other electrolyte
disturbances (hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia) can
cause seizure and are reported as complication of malnutrition.
Flashcard 1 (Q546):
• Question: The followings are possible clinical signs of malnutrition. Except:
o a. Bradycardia
o b. Normal development.
o c. Loss of knee and ankle reflexes
o d. Chvostek or Trousseau sign
o e. Hepatomegaly with fatty liver.
• Answer: B
• Explanation: In cases of malnutrition, especially prolonged malnutrition, we
typically see impaired growth and developmental delays. Therefore, "normal
development" would not be consistent with malnutrition. (See also clinical
signs table provided in OCR page 283).
Flashcard 2 (Q550):
• Question: A mother wishes to breast-feed her newborn infant, but is worried
about medical conditions that would prohibit her from doing so. You counsel
her that of her listed conditions, which of the following is a contraindication
to breast-feeding?
--- Page 14 ---
o a. Upper respiratory tract infection
o b. Cracked and bleeding nipples
o c. Mastitis
o d. Inverted nipples
o e. HIV infection
• Answer: E
• Explanation: HIV infection is a contraindication to breastfeeding in
developed countries. The risk of transmitting HIV through breast milk poses a
significant concern, and alternative feeding methods are recommended.
Flashcard 3 (Q568):
• Question: Which of the following is most common cause of Uncojugated
non hemolytic neonatal Jaundice
o A. Hypothyroidism
o B. Pyloric stenosis
o C. Breast milk Jaundice
o D. Gilbert syndrome
• Answer: C
• Explanation: Breast milk jaundice typically appears in the second week of
life and can persist for several weeks. It is caused by substances in breast
milk that inhibit the conjugation of bilirubin or increase enterohepatic
circulation of bilirubin. Gilbert syndrome is another cause of Uncojugated
non hemolytic neonatal Jaundice but it mostly presents in the second
decade of life with only few patients present with neonatal periods.
Flashcard 4 (Q583):
• Question: Characteristic features of kwashiorkor include all the following
except
o A-Moon face
o B-Dermatitis
o C-Sparse hair
--- Page 15 ---
o D-Voracious appetite
• Answer: D
• Explanation: Kwashiorkor is a form of severe protein-energy malnutrition. It
is characterized by several clinical features, but a voracious appetite is not
one of them. In fact, children with kwashiorkor often exhibit anorexia (lack of
appetite) rather than a voracious appetite. (See also clinical signs table
provided in OCR page 283).
Flashcard 5 (Q589):
• Question: All the following are causes of Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
Except
o A. Physiological Jaundice
o B. Breastfeeding jaundice
o C. ABO incompatibility
o D. Biliary atresia
• Answer: D
• Explanation: Biliary atresia is a condition that causes conjugated
hyperbilirubinemia. In biliary atresia, the bile ducts are malformed or absent,
which leads to a blockage in bile flow. This results in the accumulation of
conjugated bilirubin in the blood, causing direct (conjugated)
hyperbilirubinemia. Breastfeeding jaundice causes unconjugated
hyperbilirubinemia.
Flashcard 6 (Q618):
• Question: Regarding nutritional rickets all the following are true Except :
o A. Low s.calcium, high s. Phosphate
o B. There is Thickening of wrist
o C. Bow leg is recognized feature
o D. Softening of the ribs may predisposes patients to atelectasis and
pneumonia
• Answer: A
--- Page 16 ---
• Explanation: In nutritional rickets, there is typically low serum calcium and
low or normal serum phosphate due to impaired vitamin D metabolism,
which affects calcium and phosphate absorption. Therefore, high serum
phosphate is not characteristic. Thickening of the wrists and bow legs are
classic physical findings in rickets. Softening of the ribs (rachitic rosary) can
predispose children to atelectasis and pneumonia due to the structural
weakness in the chest wall.
